result.markdown:
---
title: "Exam Data Visualisation"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(readr) 
library(ggplot2) 
library(dplyr)
```
:::

This does three things:

- `readr` reads the CSV
- `ggplot2` makes the charts
- `dplyr` helps with simple data handling

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Step 3: Read the CSV file

Add this next chunk:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Rows: 322 Columns: 7
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (4): ID, CLASS, GENDER, RACE
dbl (3): ENGLISH, MATHS, SCIENCE

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
```


:::
:::

This assumes your Quarto file and your CSV are in the **same folder**.

If your CSV is in another folder, then you need the correct file path instead.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Step 4: Preview the data

Add this:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
head(exam_data)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 6 × 7
  ID         CLASS GENDER RACE    ENGLISH MATHS SCIENCE
  <chr>      <chr> <chr>  <chr>     <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>
1 Student321 3I    Male   Malay        21     9      15
2 Student305 3I    Female Malay        24    22      16
3 Student289 3H    Male   Chinese      26    16      16
4 Student227 3F    Male   Chinese      27    77      31
5 Student318 3I    Male   Malay        27    11      25
6 Student306 3I    Female Malay        31    16      16
```


:::
:::

This lets you see the first few rows and confirm the file loaded properly.

You can also check the column names:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
colnames(exam_data)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "ID"      "CLASS"   "GENDER"  "RACE"    "ENGLISH" "MATHS"   "SCIENCE"
```


:::
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Step 5: Make your first simple visualisation

## Bar chart of gender

Now let’s start with the easiest plot.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER)) + geom_bar() + labs( 
  title = "Number of Students by Gender", 
  x = "Gender", 
  y = "Count" )
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

### What this does

-   `ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER))` says: use `exam_data` and put `GENDER` on the x-axis
-   `geom_bar()` automatically counts how many students are in each gender category
-   `labs()` adds a chart title and axis labels

# What you should expect

This will produce a **bar chart** with: 

- one bar for **Male** 
- one bar for **Female**

Since your dataset has both genders, this is a good first plot to understand category counts.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Step 6: Make it look a bit nicer

You can slightly improve the appearance:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER, fill = GENDER)) + geom_bar() + labs( 
  title = "Number of Students by Gender", 
  x = "Gender", 
  y = "Count" ) + 
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

### New parts here

-   `fill = GENDER` gives different colours to each gender bar
-   `theme_minimal()` gives a cleaner style

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Step 7: Render or preview

Now click: 

- **Render** or 
- **Preview** in RStudio.

You should now see your first chart in the output.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Step 8: Try a second bar chart

## Bar chart of race

Now use the same idea for `RACE`:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = RACE, fill = RACE)) + geom_bar() + labs( 
  title = "Number of Students by Race", 
  x = "Race", 
  y = "Count" ) + 
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

This shows how many students belong to each race category.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Step 9: First numerical visualisation

## Histogram of Maths scores

Now let’s visualise a numerical variable.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) + geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") + labs( 
  title = "Distribution of Maths Scores", 
  x = "Maths Score", 
  y = "Frequency" ) + 
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

### What this does

-   `x = MATHS` puts maths marks on the x-axis
-   `geom_histogram()` groups the scores into intervals
-   `binwidth = 10` means the bars cover ranges like 0–10, 10–20, 20–30, and so on

This helps you see: 

-   Where most students’ maths scores lie 
-   Whether the distribution is spread out 
-   Whether there are very low or very high scores

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Step 10: Another useful plot

## Boxplot of Maths by Gender

Now let’s compare marks across categories.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER, y = MATHS, fill = GENDER)) + geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) + labs( 
  title = "Maths Scores by Gender", 
  x = "Gender", 
  y = "Maths Score" ) + 
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

### Why this is useful

A boxplot helps you compare: 

-   Median score 
-   Spread of scores 
-   Possible outliers

So instead of just counting students, you are now comparing their performance.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Step 11: Scatter plot

## English vs Maths

Now a relationship plot:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = ENGLISH, y = MATHS)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "English vs Maths Scores",
    x = "English Score",
    y = "Maths Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

This helps answer: 

-   Do students who score higher in English also tend to score higher in Maths? 
-   Is there a visible positive trend?

------------------------------------------------------------------------
